1: מַשְׂכִּיל לְדָוִד (maskîl lĕdāwid) — the superscription identifies this as a maskîl: a teaching/wisdom psalm (from śākal: to be wise, to act prudently). It is the first of the seven traditional "Penitential Psalms." אַשְׁרֵי נְשׂוּי-פֶּשַׁע (ʾashrê nĕśûy-peshaʿ, "Blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven") — nāśāʾ (passive participle: lifted, carried away): the transgression (peshaʿ: willful rebellion) is carried away like a burden. This is the language of Isa 53:4-6 (the Servant "bore our transgressions"). כְּסוּי חֲטָאָה (kĕsûy ḥaṭṭāʾt, "whose sin is covered") — kāsāh: to cover, to clothe — the sin hidden as if beneath a garment. Paul quotes vv1-2 in Rom 4:7-8 as proof that the OT itself taught justification by grace through non-reckoning of sin.
2: אַשְׁרֵי אָדָם לֹא-יַחְשֹׁב יְהוָה לוֹ עָוֹן (ʾashrê ʾādām lōʾ-yaḥshōb YHWH lô ʿāwôn, "Blessed is the man to whom the LORD does not reckon iniquity") — ḥāshab: to reckon, to account, to calculate (an accounting term). Paul highlights this word in Rom 4:3-8 — it is the same word used for God "reckoning" righteousness to Abraham (Gen 15:6). The three terms of human guilt: peshaʿ (willful rebellion), ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/missing the mark), ʿāwôn (iniquity/guilt). The three terms of divine remedy: carried away, covered, not-reckoned. וְאֵין בְּרוּחוֹ רְמִיָּה (wĕʾên bĕrûḥô rĕmiyyāh, "in whose spirit there is no deceit") — rĕmiyyāh: deceit, guile — the absence of self-deception (contrast v3-4).
3: כִּי-הֶחֱשֵׁיתִי בָּלוּ עֲצָמָי (kî-heḥĕshêtî bālû ʿăṣāmāy, "When I kept silent about my sin, my bones wasted away") — ḥāshah (hiphil: to be silent) about the sin. Bālāh: to waste away, to deteriorate (as an old garment wears out). The bones as the structural framework: unconfessed guilt decays the deepest structural level. בְּשַׁאֲגָתִי כָּל-הַיּוֹם (bĕshaʾăgātî kol-hayyôm, "through my constant groaning") — shaʾăgāh: the roaring/groaning of an animal in distress (used of the lion's roar, Prov 19:12). Inarticulate bodily distress — the body expressing what the mouth refuses to confess.
4: כִּי יוֹמָם וָלַיְלָה תִּכְבַּד עָלַי יָדֶךָ (kî yômām wālaylāh tikkbad ʿālay yādekā, "Day and night your hand pressed down on me") — kābad: to be heavy — the hand of God as a physical weight pressing on the unconfessed. Day and night without relief. נֶהְפַּךְ לְשַׁד-בְּחַרְבֹנֵי קָיִץ (nehpak lĕshad-bĕḥarbōnê qāyiṣ, "my strength dried up like moisture in the summer heat") — shad: moisture, sap (the liquid vitality of a plant); ḥarbōnê qāyiṣ: "summer drought conditions." Guilt as a drought that desiccates the inner life.
5: חַטָּאתִי אוֹדִיעֲךָ (ḥaṭṭāʾtî ʾôdîʿăkā, "I made my sin known to you") — yādaʿ (hiphil: to cause to know): the Psalmist's act of confession makes the known-to-God sin also known from his own side. וַעֲוֹנִי לֹא-כִסִּיתִי (waʿăwōnî lōʾ-kissîtî, "did not hide my iniquity") — no longer self-concealing (contrast v3: the silent hiding). וְאַתָּה נָשָׂאתָ עֲוֹן חַטָּאתִי סֶלָה (wĕʾattāh nāśāʾtā ʿāwôn ḥaṭṭāʾtî selāh, "and you forgave the guilt of my sin") — nāśāʾ: the carrying-away of the iniquity (as in v1). The full arc in one verse: I made known → you carried away. The symmetry of confession and forgiveness.
6: עַל-זֹאת יִתְפַּלֵּל כָּל-חָסִיד אֵלֶיךָ (ʿal-zōʾt yitpallêl kol-ḥāsîd ʾêlêkā, "Therefore let every faithful person pray to you") — the experience of vv3-5 becomes warrant for communal instruction: because confession works, every ḥāsîd should pray. לְעֵת מְצֹא (lĕʿêt mĕṣōʾ, "while you may still be found") — the urgency of the temporal window (māṣāʾ: to find — cf. Isa 55:6: "Seek the LORD while he may be found"). רַק לְשֵׁטֶף מַיִם רַבִּים (raq lĕshêṭep māyim rabbîm, "when the great floodwaters surge") — the māyim rabbîm (many waters) as chaos-force: prayer now offers protection when the floods come.
7: אַתָּה סֵתֶר לִי (ʾattāh sêter lî, "You are my hiding place") — the sêter (shelter/concealment) used positively (as in Ps 31:20): God as the place of protected hiddenness. Not the hiding of unconfessed sin (v3) but the sheltering of the confessed and forgiven. רָנֵּי פַלֵּט תְּסוֹבְבֵנִי (rannê palêṭ tĕsôbĕbênî, "you surround me with songs of rescue") — rannê palêṭ: "shouts of rescue/deliverance" encircling the Psalmist. The surrounding terror (Ps 31:13) replaced by surrounding songs of rescue.
8: אַשְׂכִּילְךָ וְאוֹרְךָ בְּדֶרֶךְ-זוּ תֵלֵךְ (ʾaskîlĕkā wĕʾôrĕkā bĕderek-zû têlêk, "I will instruct you and show you the way you should walk") — the subject shifts: this is now God speaking (or a wisdom teacher). Śākal (hiphil: to give insight, to make wise — the same root as maskîl in the superscription). The maskîl psalm demonstrates its didactic purpose: the God who forgave now instructs. אִיעֲצָה עָלֶיךָ עֵינִי (îʿăṣāh ʿālêkā ʿênî, "I will guide you with my eye on you") — the guiding eye as the attentive regard that keeps the led one on course.
9: אַל-תִּהְיוּ כְּסוּס כְּפֶרֶד אֵין הָבִין (ʾal-tihyû kĕsûs kĕpered ʾên hābîn, "Do not be like a horse or mule that lacks understanding") — bîn: understanding (the same word as Ps 28:5: "they take no account"). The animal that cannot self-direct and must be controlled externally contrasts with the person who responds to the divine eye's guidance without requiring the bit (beṣem) and bridle (resen) of external compulsion.
10: רַבִּים מַכְאוֹבִים לָרָשָׁע (rabbîm makʾôbîm lārāshāʿ, "The wicked have many sorrows") — the antithetical proverb: many pains (makʾôb: pain, sorrow) for the wicked vs. וְהַבּוֹטֵחַ בַּיהוָה חֶסֶד יְסוֹבְבֶנּוּ (wĕhabbōṭêaḥ baYHWH ḥesed yĕsôbĕbennû, "but steadfast love surrounds those who trust in the LORD") — surrounding ḥesed (covenant love) encircles those who bāṭaḥ (trust/lean). The contrast is spatial: many pains press on the wicked; covenant love encircles the trusting.
11: שִׂמְחוּ בַיהוָה וְגִילוּ צַדִּיקִים (śimḥû baYHWH wĕgîlû ṣaddîqîm, "Be glad in the LORD and rejoice, you righteous people!") — śāmaḥ and gîl (the twin joy-verbs). Ṣaddîqîm: the righteous — here, those declared righteous through the confession-and-forgiveness process of vv1-5 (the Pauline reading: the righteous are the forgiven-and-not-reckoned-against). וְהַרְנִינוּ כָּל-יִשְׁרֵי-לֵב (wĕharnînû kol-yishrê-lêb, "Shout for joy, all who are upright in heart!") — yāshar (upright, straight-hearted): those whose inner orientation is without the deceit of v2b — the forgiven who no longer hide.
